paragraphs.text: In the 1860s off the coast of California the famous story of the "forgotten woman of San Nicolas" arose. In 1835 the Indian tribe [on] Ghalasat decided to abandon the land of their ancestors--the small island 'San Nicolas' along the southern coast of California. Now it's difficult to say what drove the Indians to take this step. Was it the constant deceit by traders who arrived there on their ships for fur commodities, or was it the predatory extermination by hunters of sea lions and seals, or could it even have been the great number of mean wild dogs that multiplied on the island? But it is known for certain that the chief of the tribe [on] Ghalasat turned to the captain of a Spanish ship with the request to take his tribe to the mainland.
In search for her brother, the Indian girl Karana jumped overboard immediately before the ship's departure and started swimming to the shore.

She found him in the abandoned settlement. But after some time a tragic incident occurred: her brother became lost during a hunt. Karana spent 18 years on her own on the island, constantly fighting for her life with the wild dogs and occupying her time by hunting and fishing. It was only in 1853 that sailors from a ship discovered the lone woman on the coast of San Nicolas Island, speaking with difficulty in a language they did not know, and they delivered her to the Catholic Mission of Santa Barbara.
There she learned from an old monk that her entire tribe had been lost during a strong storm...
